original_abstract:
This book provides a comprehensive analysis of the European Union’s ambitious project to reform international investment law. By examining the EU's unique constitutional framework alongside its distinct reform strategies, the author explores how the concepts of the rule of law and political legitimacy serve as the primary drivers for change. 

The study highlights a critical tension: while the EU seeks to restore the legitimacy of this legal discipline by aligning it with rule-of-law principles, its practical implementation faces significant hurdles. Integrating both substantive protections—such as fair and equitable treatment and expropriation standards—and procedural innovations within intra-EU and external contexts, the analysis offers a critical evaluation of the EU's vision. Ultimately, the book assesses whether this institutional shift truly enhances the development of international investment law or if the reform approaches create new challenges in practice.
humanized_abstract:
The European Union's Investments in International Treaty Reform (IRER) Series: Vol. 1 Book Summary For the first time, this book examines the EU’s ambitious project to reform international investment law (IIL) in its entirety. Going beyond existing general comparative approaches, it does so by bringing the EU’s constitutional context as well as its various reform-oriented tactics into focus. Building on recent scholarship about the rule of law and political legitimacy at the global level and in the context of investment law, it delves into the actual drivers of present IIL reform attempts.

EU development policy currently integrates a new legal discipline based on the rule of law, known as international investment law (IIL). This paper explores the challenge of having a sector of IIL legitimized on the basis of the rule of law, while it is constantly being put into practice at the international level. This practice includes not only the substantive rules, such as the fair and equitable treatment standard and the expropriation standard, but also the new procedures introduced through recent free trade agreements as well as within the intra-EU context. This paper therefore critically assesses the relevance of this recent institutional reform in the development of IIL, including the potential pitfalls that new reform policies may give rise to in the practice of IIL, hence considering the impact of these developments on EU development policy more broadly. The book takes a pluralist perspective in order to analyse these significant changes, by examining through various empirical analyses the relevance of IIL to the development of contemporary societies at the national level, EU level and the global level.
